title: Mechanical Scaling Laws and Deformation Behavior of Nanoporous Tantalum Microparticles
authors: J. I. Ramallo, N. Vázquez von Bibow, M. A. Monclús, I. McCue, M. C. Fuertes, C. J. Ruestes
abstract: The mechanical scaling laws of dealloyed nanoporous metals depart from classical Gibson-Ashby predictions for open-cell foams due to a decreased connectivity in their solid network. However, these scaling relations have been established almost exclusively on nanoporous gold produced by electrochemical dealloying, and it is an outstanding question whether the relations apply to nanoporous networks fabricated by other dealloying methods. Here, we investigate the mechanical response of single-crystalline nanoporous tantalum (np-Ta) produced by liquid metal dealloying (LMD) a TiTa alloy in molten CuBi. Nanoindentation of individual microparticles yields an elastic modulus of 10-30 GPa and a hardness of 0.3-1.1 GPa, both scaling with the solid volume fraction in agreement with Gibson-Ashby predictions. This stiffness-density response of np-Ta departs from previous reports on nanoporous gold and is attributed to enhanced ligament connectivity enabled by the thermodynamics of the CuBi metal bath. Molecular dynamics simulations reveal dislocation-dominated plasticity during indentation of np-Ta, consistent with scanning electron microscopy observations of limited densification beneath the indents, ruling out unusual deformation mechanisms as an origin of the observed scaling. These findings identify solvent chemistry in LMD as a tunable lever for ligament connectivity, and thus for the mechanical response of nanoporous metals.
summary: This paper examines the mechanical properties of nanoporous tantalum created through a liquid metal dealloying process. It finds that unlike nanoporous gold, these tantalum particles follow standard Gibson-Ashby scaling laws because the specific solvent chemistry leads to higher ligament connectivity. Molecular dynamics simulations confirm that the deformation is dominated by dislocation-driven plasticity rather than unusual mechanisms. This work demonstrates that solvent chemistry can be used as a tool to tune the structural topology and mechanical response of nanoporous metals.
main_problem: Investigating whether the mechanical scaling laws established for electrochemically dealloyed nanoporous gold apply to nanoporous tantalum produced via liquid metal dealloying (LMD).
main_result: The stiffness and hardness of np-Ta follow Gibson-Ashby predictions due to enhanced ligament connectivity from the CuBi solvent, with plasticity driven by dislocation nucleation.
method: Experimental nanoindentation and SEM/XRD characterization combined with Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations using the LAMMPS code.
model_or_system: Single-crystalline nanoporous tantalum (np-Ta) microparticles fabricated via liquid metal dealloying of a TiTa alloy in a molten CuBi bath.
key_observables: Elastic modulus (E), hardness (H), solid volume fraction (phi), and dislocation types.
important_parameters: Ligament diameter (~200 nm), solid volume fraction (~0.35), and indentation depth (1000 nm).
main_assumptions: The solid volume fraction is estimated via a 2D SEM proxy, and Poisson's ratio is assumed to be 0.18.
figures_summary: Fig 1: SEM images and XRD patterns; Fig 2: Nanoindentation load-displacement and depth-dependent modulus; Fig 3: Hardness vs. modulus scatterplots; Fig 5: MD simulation of dislocation nucleation and strain distribution; Fig 6: Comparison of scaled modulus across different nanoporous metals.
paper_structure: Introduction of the scaling law problem, description of LMD fabrication and characterization, presentation of nanoindentation results and scaling analysis, MD simulation of deformation mechanisms, and comparison with other nanoporous metals.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: A. SEM image of typical np-Ta particles. B. SEM image of the surface showing the nanoporous bicontinuous structure of one particle. C. XRD pattern of the np-Ta powder sample. D. Particles deposited on top of a Si wafer using CrystalBond ® 509 (bright phase bounding particles) for nanoindentation testing.

Figure 2: A. Continuous stiffness measurement (CSM) load–displacement curve. B. Depth-dependent averages for the elastic modulus E under CSM. C. Representative nanoindentation curves up to 1000 nm depth. Color coding corresponds to different np-Ta particles. Inset- Top-view SEM image of a residual nanoindentation imprint.

Figure 3: Hardness (H) vs elastic modulus (E) values compared with a power law dependence based on equation 4. The experimental uncertainty in E and H is approximately 10%. Red line corresponds to n = 3/2 while the green line corresponds to n = 2.

Figure 4: Scaling laws predictions for E (Eq. 1) and H (3) compared with histograms of nanoindentation results. Dashed lines correspond to the median of values, while dotted lines correspond to limiting values. Note that the median of histogram data closely aligns with the measured solid fraction.

Figure 5: A. Setup of the simulated np-Ta system. B. The penetration of the indenter induces the nucleation of dislocations on the ligament surfaces and their propagation inside the ligaments (red arrow). C. Cross-section indicating the displacement magnitude profile of the ligaments at a penetration depth of 6 nm, together with glissile (green lines) and sessile (pink) dislocations. Transparency was applied to atoms in the interior of ligaments to enhance visualization of dislocations. D. Shear strain distribution at a penetration depth of 6 nm. Arrows indicate planar shear localization, consistent with dislocation activity. Bulkier shear distribution (white ellipse) corresponds to a twin,...

Figure 6: Scaled elastic modulus. np-Ta results (this work) are scattered in a region close to the predicted by a standard Gibson-Ashby (std G-A) equation. Triangle symbols indicate nanoindentation results, whereas square symbols indicate micro-compression and/or micro-tension results.
